steps.title: Identify the pattern and the blank's job
steps.content: For ten million years after the dinosaurs' extinction, low competition meant bodies grew faster than brains — the brain-to-body ratio shrank. The passage then states that as populations swelled, competition intensified. The established principle is that competition favors intelligence and large, complex brains. The blank must describe what intensified competition would logically produce: the reversal of the earlier shrinkage.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — favored an evolutionary increase in brain size relative to body size among mammals.: This is the exact reversal the principle predicts — competition drives brain size up relative to body size, undoing the earlier shrinkage. ✓
  • Choice B — restricted resources so drastically that mammals struggled to secure enough food to maintain large brain sizes.: The passage focuses on competition favoring larger brains, not starvation shrinking them; this introduces an unsupported mechanism. ✗
  • Choice C — encouraged mammals with large brain sizes to adapt to a range of ecological niches.: Ecological niche adaptation was the earlier phase (post-dinosaur expansion), not the result of intensified competition; this conflates two different periods. ✗
  • Choice D — heightened the advantage that large body size conferred on mammals in certain ecological niches.: This contradicts the principle — competition is said to favor brain size, not body size. ✗
That matches Choice A — favored an evolutionary increase in brain size relative to body size among mammals..
